old_claim.note: Fire Control is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: The documented method requires a heated water bath or a stove with mild charcoal fire to vaporize the charge; when the bath cools, distillation ceases.

source_shape.source_locator: pp. 88-89 describe hot-water-bath distillation and a purpose-built stove with mild charcoal fire, state that cooling stops distillation, and warn that excessive fire destroys fragrance and taste.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Controlled heat is an operating step of the alembic procedure, not merely distant pyrotechnic ancestry.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The edge is required because without sustained controlled heating the liquid does not vaporize and the cited procedure explicitly stops.

ontology_before: Fire control was modeled only as deep historical lineage despite being part of the operating mechanism.
ontology_after: Controlled fire and heat are modeled as a required distillation method component, with the specific water-bath and charcoal-fire procedure documented.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-medieval-alembic-distillation-scope: fire_control is required for the scoped heated-distillation method.
would_reject_if: The node is broadened to include non-thermal separation methods.
A dedicated controlled_process_heating node is added and should replace fire_control.
A stronger source shows the cited apparatus operated without an external heat source.
short_reason: Alembic distillation stops without controlled heating.
